summary: DHH argues against the recurring industry dream of building software from reusable, off-the-shelf components. He responds to proposals that envision shopping for finished components and configuring them into complete systems, calling this a 'software mirage.' He contends that software is fundamentally a collection of design decisions that can't be reduced to interchangeable parts like hardware ICs. While infrastructure below the business domain (like Rails) can and should be standardized, business-level components resist meaningful reuse. The real solution, he argues, is design patterns rather than prefabricated components.
key_points: Software is a collection of design decisions, not interchangeable hardware-like parts, making high-level component reuse a persistent false hope
The components dream parallels failed approaches like 4GLs, CASE tools, and model-driven architecture that tried to industrialize programming
Infrastructure below the business domain can be standardized (as Rails demonstrates), but business-level design decisions cannot
The real solution to repeating problems is patterns—gathering and describing forces, challenges, and proven solutions—not embodying them in components
Pre-made applications work only when the problem space is drastically reduced and already well-known
money_quotes: Software is not like hardware, high-level components can't be reduced to interchangeable IC's.
Software is a collection of design decisions and where that cut should be made, which decisions we should bother with and which we shouldn't, is ever fluctuating.
The components dream is in many ways similar to the aspirations of model-driven architecture. Programming is a soon to be lost art of craftmanship, move over for industrialization!
Only it never really works out that way, does it?
The solution is not to embody repeating business problems in components, but to look for patterns.
The border at which new functionality is accepted or rejected lies at the brink of the business domain.
I don't believe that user management, access control, discussion boards, or Bill of Materials classes make for good component candidates.
key_insight: Software's value lies in its design decisions, which are too context-dependent to package as reusable components; the real leverage comes from standardizing infrastructure and sharing patterns, not prefabricated business logic.
